The editor will be $EDITOR, or `nano`\r\nif that's not defined (`Notepad` on Windows).\r\n\r\nUsage: jj describe [OPTIONS] [REVSETS]...\r\n\r\nArguments:\r\n  [REVSETS]...\r\n          The revision(s) whose description to edit (default: @) [aliases: -r]\r\n\r\nOptions:\r\n  -m, --message <MESSAGE>\r\n          The change description to use (don't open editor)\r\n          \r\n          If multiple revisions are specified, the same description will be used for all of them.\r\n\r\n      --stdin\r\n          Read the change description from stdin\r\n          \r\n          If multiple revisions are specified, the same description will be used for all of them.\r\n\r\n      --editor\r\n          Open an editor to edit the change description\r\n          \r\n          Forces an editor to open when using `--stdin` or `--message` to allow the message to be\r\n          edited afterwards.\r\n\r\n  -h, --help\r\n          Print help (see a summary with '-h')\r\n\r\nGlobal Options:\r\n  -R, --repository <REPOSITORY>\r\n          Path to repository to operate on\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu searches for the closest .jj/ directory in an ancestor of the current\r\n          working directory.\r\n\r\n      --ignore-working-copy\r\n          Don't snapshot the working copy, and don't update it\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu snapshots the working copy at the beginning of every command. The\r\n          working copy is also updated at the end of the command, if the command modified the\r\n          working-copy commit (`@`). If you want to avoid snapshotting the working copy and instead\r\n          see a possibly stale working-copy commit, you can use `--ignore-working-copy`. This may be\r\n          useful e.g. in a command prompt, especially if you have another process that commits the\r\n          working copy.\r\n          \r\n          Loading the repository at a specific operation with `--at-operation` implies\r\n          `--ignore-working-copy`.\r\n\r\n      --ignore-immutable\r\n          Allow rewriting immutable commits\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu prevents rewriting commits in the configured set of immutable commits.\r\n          This option disables that check and lets you rewrite any commit but the root commit.\r\n          \r\n          This option only affects the check. It does not affect the `immutable_heads()` revset or\r\n          the `immutable` template keyword.\r\n\r\n      --at-operation <AT_OPERATION>\r\n          Operation to load the repo at\r\n          \r\n          Operation to load the repo at. By default, Jujutsu loads the repo at the most recent\r\n          operation, or at the merge of the divergent operations if any.\r\n          \r\n          You can use `--at-op=<operation ID>` to see what the repo looked like at an earlier\r\n          operation. For example `jj --at-op=<operation ID> st` will show you what `jj st` would\r\n          have shown you when the given operation had just finished. `--at-op=@` is pretty much the\r\n          same as the default except that divergent operations will never be merged.\r\n          \r\n          Use `jj op log` to find the operation ID you want. Any unambiguous prefix of the operation\r\n          ID is enough.\r\n          \r\n          When loading the repo at an earlier operation, the working copy will be ignored, as if\r\n          `--ignore-working-copy` had been specified.\r\n          \r\n          It is possible to run mutating commands when loading the repo at an earlier operation.\r\n          Doing that is equivalent to having run concurrent commands starting at the earlier\r\n          operation. There's rarely a reason to do that, but it is possible.\r\n          \r\n          [aliases: --at-op]\r\n\r\n      --debug\r\n          Enable debug logging\r\n\r\n      --color <WHEN>\r\n          When to colorize output\r\n          \r\n          [possible values: always, never, debug, auto]\r\n\r\n      --quiet\r\n          Silence non-primary command output\r\n          \r\n          For example, `jj file list` will still list files, but it won't tell you if the working\r\n          copy was snapshotted or if descendants were rebased.\r\n          \r\n          Warnings and errors will still be printed.\r\n\r\n      --no-pager\r\n          Disable the pager\r\n\r\n      --config <NAME=VALUE>\r\n          Additional configuration options (can be repeated)\r\n          \r\n          The name should be specified as TOML dotted keys. The value should be specified as a TOML\r\n          expression. help"},"output":{"output":"Jujutsu (An experimental VCS)\r\n\r\nTo get started, see the tutorial [`jj help -k tutorial`].\r\n\r\n[`jj help -k tutorial`]: https://docs.jj-vcs.dev/latest/tutorial/\r\n\r\nUsage: jj [OPTIONS] [COMMAND]\r\n\r\nCommands:\r\n  abandon           Abandon a revision\r\n  absorb            Move changes from a revision into the stack of mutable revisions\r\n  bisect            Find a bad revision by bisection\r\n  bookmark          Manage bookmarks [default alias: b]\r\n  commit            Update the description and create a new change on top [default alias: ci]\r\n  config            Manage config options\r\n  describe          Update the change description or other metadata [default alias: desc]\r\n  diff              Compare file contents between two revisions\r\n  diffedit          Touch up the content changes in a revision with a diff editor\r\n  duplicate         Create new changes with the same content as existing ones\r\n  edit              Sets the specified revision as the working-copy revision\r\n  evolog            Show how a change has evolved over time [aliases: evolution-log]\r\n  file              File operations\r\n  fix               Update files with formatting fixes or other changes\r\n  gerrit            Interact with Gerrit Code Review\r\n  git               Commands for working with Git remotes and the underlying Git repo\r\n  help              Print this message or the help of the given subcommand(s)\r\n  interdiff         Show differences between the diffs of two revisions\r\n  log               Show revision history\r\n  metaedit          Modify the metadata of a revision without changing its content\r\n  new               Create a new, empty change and (by default) edit it in the working copy\r\n  next              Move the working-copy commit to the child revision\r\n  operation         Commands for working with the operation log [aliases: op]\r\n  parallelize       Parallelize revisions by making them siblings\r\n  prev              Change the working copy revision relative to the parent revision\r\n  rebase            Move revisions to different parent(s)\r\n  redo              Redo the most recently undone operation\r\n  resolve           Resolve conflicted files with an external merge tool\r\n  restore           Restore paths from another revision\r\n  revert            Apply the reverse of the given revision(s)\r\n  root              Show the current workspace root directory (shortcut for `jj workspace root`)\r\n  show              Show commit description and changes in a revision\r\n  sign              Cryptographically sign a revision\r\n  simplify-parents  Simplify parent edges for the specified revision(s)\r\n  sparse            Manage which paths from the working-copy commit are present in the working copy\r\n  split             Split a revision in two\r\n  squash            Move changes from a revision into another revision\r\n  status            Show high-level repo status [default alias: st]\r\n  tag               Manage tags\r\n  undo              Undo the last operation\r\n  unsign            Drop a cryptographic signature\r\n  util              Infrequently used commands such as for generating shell completions\r\n  version           Display version information\r\n  workspace         Commands for working with workspaces\r\n\r\nOptions:\r\n  -h, --help\r\n          Print help (see a summary with '-h')\r\n\r\n  -V, --version\r\n          Print version\r\n\r\nGlobal Options:\r\n  -R, --repository <REPOSITORY>\r\n          Path to repository to operate on\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu searches for the closest .jj/ directory in an ancestor of the current\r\n          working directory.\r\n\r\n      --ignore-working-copy\r\n          Don't snapshot the working copy, and don't update it\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu snapshots the working copy at the beginning of every command. The\r\n          working copy is also updated at the end of the command, if the command modified the\r\n          working-copy commit (`@`). If you want to avoid snapshotting the working copy and instead\r\n          see a possibly stale working-copy commit, you can use `--ignore-working-copy`. This may be\r\n          useful e.g. in a command prompt, especially if you have another process that commits the\r\n          working copy.\r\n          \r\n          Loading the repository at a specific operation with `--at-operation` implies\r\n          `--ignore-working-copy`.\r\n\r\n      --ignore-immutable\r\n          Allow rewriting immutable commits\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu prevents rewriting commits in the configured set of immutable commits.\r\n          This option disables that check and lets you rewrite any commit but the root commit.\r\n          \r\n          This option only affects the check. It does not affect the `immutable_heads()` revset or\r\n          the `immutable` template keyword.\r\n\r\n      --at-operation <AT_OPERATION>\r\n          Operation to load the repo at\r\n          \r\n          Operation to load the repo at. By default, Jujutsu loads the repo at the most recent\r\n          operation, or at the merge of the divergent operations if any.\r\n          \r\n          You can use `--at-op=<operation ID>` to see what the repo looked like at an earlier\r\n          operation. For example `jj --at-op=<operation ID> st` will show you what `jj st` would\r\n          have shown you when the given operation had just finished. `--at-op=@` is pretty much the\r\n          same as the default except that divergent operations will never be merged.\r\n          \r\n          Use `jj op log` to find the operation ID you want. Any unambiguous prefix of the operation\r\n          ID is enough.\r\n          \r\n          When loading the repo at an earlier operation, the working copy will be ignored, as if\r\n          `--ignore-working-copy` had been specified.\r\n          \r\n          It is possible to run mutating commands when loading the repo at an earlier operation.\r\n          Doing that is equivalent to having run concurrent commands starting at the earlier\r\n          operation. There's rarely a reason to do that, but it is possible.\r\n          \r\n          [aliases: --at-op]\r\n\r\n      --debug\r\n          Enable debug logging\r\n\r\n      --color <WHEN>\r\n          When to colorize output\r\n          \r\n          [possible values: always, never, debug, auto]\r\n\r\n      --quiet\r\n          Silence non-primary command output\r\n          \r\n          For example, `jj file list` will still list files, but it won't tell you if the working\r\n          copy was snapshotted or if descendants were rebased.\r\n          \r\n          Warnings and errors will still be printed.\r\n\r\n      --no-pager\r\n          Disable the pager\r\n\r\n      --config <NAME=VALUE>\r\n          Additional configuration options (can be repeated)\r\n          \r\n          The name should be specified as TOML dotted keys. The value should be specified as a TOML\r\n          expression. The name and email may come from the `JJ_USER` and `JJ_EMAIL` environment\r\nvariables, as well as by passing `--config user.name` and `--config user.email`.\r\n\r\nUsage: jj metaedit [OPTIONS] [REVSETS]...\r\n\r\nArguments:\r\n  [REVSETS]...\r\n          The revision(s) to modify (default: @) [aliases: -r]\r\n\r\nOptions:\r\n      --update-change-id\r\n          Generate a new change-id\r\n          \r\n          This generates a new change-id for the revision.\r\n\r\n  -m, --message <MESSAGE>\r\n          Update the change description\r\n          \r\n          This updates the change description, without opening the editor.\r\n          \r\n          Use `jj describe` if you want to use an editor.\r\n\r\n      --update-author-timestamp\r\n          Update the author timestamp\r\n          \r\n          This updates the author date to the current time, without modifying the author.\r\n\r\n      --update-author\r\n          Update the author to the configured user\r\n          \r\n          This updates the author name and email. The author timestamp is not modified – use\r\n          --update-author-timestamp to update the author timestamp.\r\n          \r\n          You can use it in combination with the JJ_USER and JJ_EMAIL environment variables to set a\r\n          different author:\r\n          \r\n          $ JJ_USER='Foo Bar' JJ_EMAIL=foo@bar.com jj metaedit --update-author\r\n\r\n      --author <AUTHOR>\r\n          Set author to the provided string\r\n          \r\n          This changes author name and email while retaining author timestamp for non-discardable\r\n          commits.\r\n          \r\n          ```shell $ jj metaedit --author \"Foo Bar <foo@bar.com>\" ```\r\n\r\n      --author-timestamp <AUTHOR_TIMESTAMP>\r\n          Set the author date to the given date\r\n          \r\n          The date can either be human readable ([RFC2822], eg 'Sun, 23 Jan 2000 01:23:45 PST') or a\r\n          time stamp ([RFC3339], eg '2000-01-23T01:23:45-08:00').\r\n          \r\n          [RFC2822]: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc2822\r\n          \r\n          [RFC3339]: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc3339\r\n\r\n      --force-rewrite\r\n          Rewrite the commit, even if no other metadata changed\r\n          \r\n          This updates the committer timestamp to the current time, as well as the committer name\r\n          and email.\r\n          \r\n          Even if this option is not passed, the committer name, email, and timestamp will be\r\n          updated if other metadata is updated. By default,\r\nthe output only includes mutable revisions, along with some additional revisions for context. Use\r\n`jj log -r ::` to see all revisions. See [`jj help -k revsets`] for information about the syntax.\r\n\r\n[`jj help -k revsets`]: https://docs.jj-vcs.dev/latest/revsets/\r\n\r\nSpans of revisions that are not included in the graph per `--revisions` are rendered as a synthetic\r\nnode labeled \"(elided revisions)\".\r\n\r\nThe working-copy commit is indicated by a `@` symbol in the graph. [Immutable revisions] have a `◆`\r\nsymbol. Other commits have a `○` symbol. All of these symbols can be [customized].\r\n\r\n[Immutable revisions]: https://docs.jj-vcs.dev/latest/config/#set-of-immutable-commits\r\n\r\n[customized]: https://docs.jj-vcs.dev/latest/config/#node-style\r\n\r\nUsage: jj log [OPTIONS] [FILESETS]...\r\n\r\nArguments:\r\n  [FILESETS]...\r\n          Show revisions modifying the given paths\r\n\r\nOptions:\r\n  -r, --revisions <REVSETS>\r\n          Which revisions to show\r\n          \r\n          If no paths nor revisions are specified, this defaults to the `revsets.log` setting.\r\n\r\n  -n, --limit <LIMIT>\r\n          Limit number of revisions to show\r\n          \r\n          Applied after revisions are filtered and reordered topologically, but before being\r\n          reversed.\r\n\r\n      --reversed\r\n          Show revisions in the opposite order (older revisions first)\r\n\r\n  -G, --no-graph\r\n          Don't show the graph, show a flat list of revisions\r\n\r\n  -T, --template <TEMPLATE>\r\n          Render each revision using the given template\r\n          \r\n          Run `jj log -T` to list the built-in templates.\r\n          \r\n          You can also specify arbitrary template expressions using the [built-in keywords]. See\r\n          [`jj help -k templates`] for more information.\r\n          \r\n          If not specified, this defaults to the `templates.log` setting.\r\n          \r\n          [built-in keywords]: https://docs.jj-vcs.dev/latest/templates/#commit-keywords\r\n          \r\n          [`jj help -k templates`]: https://docs.jj-vcs.dev/latest/templates/\r\n\r\n  -p, --patch\r\n          Show patch\r\n\r\n      --count\r\n          Print the number of commits instead of showing them\r\n\r\n  -h, --help\r\n          Print help (see a summary with '-h')\r\n\r\nDiff Formatting Options:\r\n  -s, --summary\r\n          For each path, show only whether it was modified, added, or deleted\r\n\r\n      --stat\r\n          Show a histogram of the changes\r\n\r\n      --types\r\n          For each path, show only its type before and after\r\n          \r\n          The diff is shown as two letters. The first letter indicates the type before and the\r\n          second letter indicates the type after. '-' indicates that the path was not present, 'F'\r\n          represents a regular file, `L' represents a symlink, 'C' represents a conflict, and 'G'\r\n          represents a Git submodule.\r\n\r\n      --name-only\r\n          For each path, show only its path\r\n          \r\n          Typically useful for shell commands like: `jj diff -r @- --name-only | xargs perl -pi\r\n          -e's/OLD/NEW/g`\r\n\r\n      --git\r\n          Show a Git-format diff\r\n\r\n      --color-words\r\n          Show a word-level diff with changes indicated only by color\r\n\r\n      --tool <TOOL>\r\n          Generate diff by external command\r\n          \r\n          A builtin format can also be specified as `:<name>`. For example, `--tool=:git` is\r\n          equivalent to `--git`.\r\n\r\n      --context <CONTEXT>\r\n          Number of lines of context to show\r\n\r\n      --ignore-all-space\r\n          Ignore whitespace when comparing lines\r\n\r\n      --ignore-space-change\r\n          Ignore changes in amount of whitespace when comparing lines\r\n\r\nGlobal Options:\r\n  -R, --repository <REPOSITORY>\r\n          Path to repository to operate on\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu searches for the closest .jj/ directory in an ancestor of the current\r\n          working directory.\r\n\r\n      --ignore-working-copy\r\n          Don't snapshot the working copy, and don't update it\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu snapshots the working copy at the beginning of every command. The\r\n          working copy is also updated at the end of the command, if the command modified the\r\n          working-copy commit (`@`). If you want to avoid snapshotting the working copy and instead\r\n          see a possibly stale working-copy commit, you can use `--ignore-working-copy`. This may be\r\n          useful e.g. in a command prompt, especially if you have another process that commits the\r\n          working copy.\r\n          \r\n          Loading the repository at a specific operation with `--at-operation` implies\r\n          `--ignore-working-copy`.\r\n\r\n      --ignore-immutable\r\n          Allow rewriting immutable commits\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu prevents rewriting commits in the configured set of immutable commits.\r\n          This option disables that check and lets you rewrite any commit but the root commit.\r\n          \r\n          This option only affects the check. It does not affect the `immutable_heads()` revset or\r\n          the `immutable` template keyword.\r\n\r\n      --at-operation <AT_OPERATION>\r\n          Operation to load the repo at\r\n          \r\n          Operation to load the repo at. By default, Jujutsu loads the repo at the most recent\r\n          operation, or at the merge of the divergent operations if any.\r\n          \r\n          You can use `--at-op=<operation ID>` to see what the repo looked like at an earlier\r\n          operation. For example `jj --at-op=<operation ID> st` will show you what `jj st` would\r\n          have shown you when the given operation had just finished. `--at-op=@` is pretty much the\r\n          same as the default except that divergent operations will never be merged.\r\n          \r\n          Use `jj op log` to find the operation ID you want. Any unambiguous prefix of the operation\r\n          ID is enough.\r\n          \r\n          When loading the repo at an earlier operation, the working copy will be ignored, as if\r\n          `--ignore-working-copy` had been specified.\r\n          \r\n          It is possible to run mutating commands when loading the repo at an earlier operation.\r\n          Doing that is equivalent to having run concurrent commands starting at the earlier\r\n          operation. There's rarely a reason to do that, but it is possible.\r\n          \r\n          [aliases: --at-op]\r\n\r\n      --debug\r\n          Enable debug logging\r\n\r\n      --color <WHEN>\r\n          When to colorize output\r\n          \r\n          [possible values: always, never, debug, auto]\r\n\r\n      --quiet\r\n          Silence non-primary command output\r\n          \r\n          For example, `jj file list` will still list files, but it won't tell you if the working\r\n          copy was snapshotted or if descendants were rebased.\r\n          \r\n          Warnings and errors will still be printed.\r\n\r\n      --no-pager\r\n          Disable the pager\r\n\r\n      --config <NAME=VALUE>\r\n          Additional configuration options (can be repeated)\r\n          \r\n          The name should be specified as TOML dotted keys. The value should be specified as a TOML\r\n          expression.
